These constraints are the underlying foundation for the stability of traditional finance. Programmable money, however, replaces all human and institutional constraints with code rules. It replaces time-difference control with instant execution rules via smart contracts; it replaces human intermediary review with embedded automated logic in transactions; and it breaks down geographical barriers to capital flows through a globally unified network. Financial risk control hasn't disappeared; it has simply migrated from institutional control to the software and code level. This rule migration has already begun in earnest. Formal endorsements from traditional financial institutions are following suit. Morgan Stanley recently launched a stablecoin reserve asset portfolio, specifically allocating underlying reserve assets for stablecoins with a compliant and highly liquid architecture; it also advised clients to allocate a small percentage of their assets to crypto assets and launched a Bitcoin spot ETF. The signal is clear: traditional finance is not standing aside from the digital asset revolution, but actively adapting and deeply integrating. The next wave of revolution is already emerging. When currency becomes programmable and flows in real time, funds will not stagnate after the initial allocation, but will enter a state of continuous dynamic management. Artificial intelligence agents can automatically handle fund flows, completing fund splitting, savings, and investment according to preset rules the moment funds arrive. After delivery personnel's income arrives in real time, a portion of the funds can be automatically transferred to investment accounts, short-term government bond funds, or other financial products. Under this system, currency not only flows faster, but also achieves intelligent and proactive management from the moment it is generated, completely blurring the boundaries between payment and asset management. For a long time, time constraints have been deeply tied to the financial system; settlement cycles, batch processing, and post-event reconciliation all rely on time buffers. In this new paradigm, however, time is no longer a rigid limitation, but a flexibly manageable option. Funds no longer need to wait for approval, have their allocation delayed, or their investment postponed; circulation, allocation, and compound interest growth are all completed instantly. The transformation that began in 2024 at the infrastructure level has now deeply penetrated the real economy and is further supported by traditional institutions. The entire financial system is not being overthrown by external forces, but rather undergoing internal restructuring and iteration. This is the true face of structural change. It won't originate from a single sudden event, but rather from a series of gradual, progressive actions: October 2024: Underlying infrastructure completed adaptation; June 2025: Leading operating companies recognize its practical value; 2026: New business models scaled up; Currently: Traditional financial institutions are fully aligning with the trend. Every decision is rational, pragmatic, and gradual, yet they all point to the same irreversible direction: Currency is becoming globalized; transactions are becoming real-time and instantaneous; and value transfer is becoming programmable. DoorDash is not merely introducing a new payment method; it is implementing a completely new financial paradigm: funds are allocated automatically and instantly based on preset rules, eliminating the need for delayed distribution. This is a fundamentally different financial system. Like all profound structural reforms, it never makes its debut with a dramatic, revolutionary gesture. At first, it seemed like just a series of insignificant, minor decisions. Until quantitative changes led to qualitative changes, and the era was completely rewritten.

data.title: Self-check: Is your company agent-friendly?
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

I. Trend: In the AI ​​Era, Comprehensive Business Integration is Unstoppable

In recent years, the global technology industry has undergone a silent but profound paradigm shift. If the past decade was the golden age of "Internet+", then the present and the next decade will be the certain future of "AI+". From the wave of large-scale models sparked by ChatGPT to the rush of various industries to deploy intelligent agents, an undeniable fact is emerging: AI is no longer a tool for adding icing on the cake, but is becoming the infrastructure for the survival and development of enterprises.

Responsibility Boundaries and Emergency Mechanisms: When the behavior of an AI agent causes losses, how should responsibility be divided? In a company's governance structure, the behavioral boundaries of the AI ​​agent, the timing of human intervention, and the post-incident handling procedures must be clearly defined.

III. Current Situation: Most Companies' Businesses Are Not Yet Ready to Connect with AI

If we examine most companies using the above four dimensions as a benchmark, we will find an embarrassing reality: companies that claim to "embrace AI" have business systems and management processes that are extremely hostile, even "antagonistic," to AI agents.

(I) From an Internal Management Perspective: The Data Swamp and the Process Maze The biggest obstacle to AI from internal management lies primarily in data quality. AI agents are not magicians; they rely on high-quality, structured, and semantically consistent data. However, the state of data within many enterprises can only be described as appalling: The same field (such as "customer name" or "product model") in different departments uses completely different naming rules and coding systems in their respective systems; Paper documents and electronic documents coexist, with a large amount of critical information locked in scanned copies, PDF images, and even audio recordings, requiring AI to perform complex OCR and speech recognition to "understand" it; Missing historical data, entry errors, and duplicate records are common, and data governance remains merely a slogan; The business processes themselves lack standardization; the same approval item may take completely different paths in different branches and with different handlers. When an AI agent attempts to access such an internal system, it faces not a clear and smooth information highway, but a data swamp full of potholes, dead ends, and dead ends. How can an AI agent that approves reimbursements automatically approve expenses if the classification logic for "travel expenses" and "transportation expenses" is inconsistent across different systems? (II) From the perspective of external business: Closed interface and arbitrary processes. External business systems also have many problems. Many companies' customer interfaces and transaction systems were not designed with the scenario of "AI agents acting as users" in mind from the outset. The lack or non-standardization of APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) is a major pain point. For example, an AI-powered procurement agent aiming to automatically compare product prices would be forced to use a "simulated browser" approach to crawl web pages if the target supplier's website lacked an open, standardized, and authenticated product information interface—a method that is fragile, inefficient, and often violates the website's terms of service. Many companies still view "external interfaces" as a technical issue rather than a strategic one, providing outdated API documentation, obsolete authentication methods, and opaque rate-limiting policies, making them extremely unfriendly to AI agents. The human-centric approach to process design is equally serious. The interfaces of most business systems are designed for human vision, human reaction time, and human attention patterns: complex multi-level menus, operation options that require "hovering" to appear, dynamically refreshed CAPTCHAs, and forced-read pop-up announcements… These designs may only be slightly inconvenient for human users, but they are insurmountable obstacles for AI agents. An AI agent aiming to automatically complete order queries will become a fragile behemoth riddled with conditional branches and exception handling when faced with a process that requires opening a three-level menu, searching for the "last 7 days" option in a vague date picker, and then encountering a satisfaction survey pop-up that may appear at any time. (III) From the perspective of ecosystem participation: fragmented efforts and lack of standards. While internal and business integration issues can be resolved through the efforts of individual companies, the challenges at the ecosystem level far exceed the control of any single enterprise. The primary problem is the heterogeneity of system architectures among enterprises. Company A's ERP and Company B's CRM have completely different underlying data models; the meanings of order status codes in one industry and logistics status codes in another are incomprehensible. When multiple intelligent agents from different enterprises attempt to collaborate on an end-to-end business process, their primary challenge is not complex business logic, but rather the fundamental problem of "translation"—a translation task that often requires extensive custom development, completely contradicting the original intention of automated collaboration. The lack or fragmentation of standards and protocols is a deeper issue. While some industry organizations and technology alliances are promoting B2B integration standards (such as certain versions of EDI, RosettaNet, OASIS, etc.), these standards are either outdated and fail to consider the autonomous interaction needs of intelligent agents; or they are too complex and costly to implement; or they have limited coverage, applicable only to specific industries or stages. Currently, an open and universal standard protocol covering the entire chain of "agent discovery - capability negotiation - data exchange - state synchronization - anomaly handling - settlement and clearing" is still lacking. Trust and security mechanisms are major obstacles to ecosystem collaboration. How can an intelligent agent from one enterprise verify the identity and authorization of another enterprise's intelligent agent? How can sensitive data during interactions be prevented from being leaked or eavesdropped on by third parties? When an intelligent agent behaves abnormally (e.g., is maliciously hijacked), how can its ecosystem access be quickly detected and severed? These problems can be partially solved under traditional API security frameworks (OAuth, API keys, mTLS, etc.), but when the interacting entity changes from an "enterprise system" to an "autonomous intelligent agent," and when the interaction mode changes from "request-response" to "multi-turn dialogue and autonomous decision-making," existing security models become inadequate. (IV) From a Governance and Compliance Perspective: Responsibility Vacuum and Regulatory Lag Compliance issues are equally worrying. Many companies, in their AI development, essentially adopt a "get it running first, then worry about the consequences" approach, with governance structures lagging significantly behind technology deployment. The attribution of responsibility for AI decisions is ambiguous—when a human resources AI agent mistakenly filters out a qualified candidate, is it the responsibility of the algorithm engineer? The business department? Or the AI ​​itself (if legally permissible)? Companies often lack clear and operational rules for assigning responsibility, ultimately resulting in no one being held accountable. The lack of ethical review mechanisms—what AI application scenarios require ethical review? Who should form the review committee? What are the review standards? Most companies simply haven't established relevant systems and processes. As a result, some AI applications with obvious risks of discrimination or privacy are launched without sufficient evaluation, only to be hastily remedied after complaints or public outcry. The "transfer mentality" of regulatory compliance—many companies directly "transfer" traditional compliance requirements to AI scenarios without understanding the new problems brought about by AI. For example, data protection regulations require users to have the right to "explain" the logic of automated decisions, but can the decision boundary of a deep neural network really be clearly "explained" in natural language? Companies often use superficial explanations such as "our model is based on three features X, Y, and Z" to evade the issue, without truly addressing the problem of explainability. IV. Action: The Inevitable Path of Comprehensive Attention Faced with the aforementioned complex situation, enterprises cannot simply passively wait for technology to mature or standards to be perfected; instead, they must proactively and systematically promote AI integration across four levels: strategy, technology, business, and compliance. (I) Strategic Level: From "Tool Thinking" to "Ecosystem Thinking" Top management needs to clearly recognize that AI is not just the next software project, but a strategic variable influencing business models and organizational structures. AI integration is not just a matter for the IT department, but for the entire company. Enterprises need to develop a clear AI strategic roadmap, specifying which business segments will introduce AI agents over the next three to five years, which agent capabilities will be developed in-house or outsourced, how to balance automation and human intervention, and how to respond to changes in the external ecosystem. More importantly, enterprises need to make a fundamental shift: from viewing AI as an internal tool for improving efficiency to viewing it as an identity carrier for participating in the external ecosystem. This means that a company's strategic planning should include considerations such as "how our agents will collaborate with agents in other organizations" and "what role we hope to play in the entire agent ecosystem." (II) Technical Aspect: Building a System Architecture "Friendly to Intelligent Agents" The technical team needs to re-examine the existing system architecture, shifting from a "human-centered" design philosophy to a "dual-center" design philosophy of "human and intelligent agent". Specifically, enterprises need to: All core business functions should be exposed through well-designed, fully documented, and version-controlled APIs, optimized for machine-to-machine call scenarios (e.g., providing batch operation interfaces, supporting asynchronous callbacks, and designing reasonable rate limiting and retry strategies). Data Readiness: Establish a unified data governance framework to ensure that key data entities (customers, products, orders, suppliers, etc.) have unique, clear, and machine-readable definitions across the company. Historical data cleaning and labeling should be treated as a strategic investment. Embedded Intelligence: Extract valuable business logic (such as approval rules, pricing strategies, and risk assessment standards) from hard-coded applications and encapsulate them into independent "capability units" that can be invoked by AI agents. This allows agents to combine these capabilities like building blocks, rather than being forced to repeatedly implement or bypass them.

Observable Design: Design sufficient logging, monitoring, and tracking capabilities for the behavior of AI agents. When multiple agents work collaboratively, it is possible to clearly trace which agent made which decision, at what time, and based on what data. This is the foundation for troubleshooting and accountability.

(III) Business Level: Redesigning Processes and Roles

Business departments cannot completely delegate AI integration to the technology department. On the contrary, the restructuring of business processes is the key to the implementation of AI.

Process Simplification and Standardization: Before integrating with AI, examine the rationality of existing processes. A process that is inherently chaotic and full of exceptions will only create more chaos after automation. Enterprises need to first streamline and standardize their business processes, reducing unnecessary branches and exceptions. Human-Machine Collaboration Model Design: Not all processes are suitable for full automation. Enterprises need to clearly define which processes will be completely handed over to AI, which require AI to process first and then human review, and which will always remain human. The key is to design smooth "transfer" and "upgrade" mechanisms, rather than simply replacing humans with AI.

Employee Empowerment: AI is not here to steal jobs, but it will certainly change the content of jobs. Enterprises need to systematically train employees, teaching them how to collaborate with AI agents, how to understand and intervene in the decision-making process of AI agents, and how to take over and handle anomalies when AI agents fail.

(IV) Compliance Level: Establishing Embedded Governance Mechanisms

Compliance should not be an afterthought, but should be embedded in the entire lifecycle of AI system design, development, deployment, and operation from day one.

Compliance should not be a post-hoc "patch", but should be embedded in the entire lifecycle of AI system design, development, deployment, and operation from day one.
